## CyberSleuth: Autonomous Blue-Team LLM Agent for Web Attack Forensics

Stefano Fumero Politecnico di Torino

Danilo Giordano Politecnico di Torino

## Abstract

Large Language Model (LLM) agents are powerful tools for automating complex tasks. In cybersecurity, researchers have primarily explored their use in red-team operations such as vulnerability discovery and penetration tests. Defensive uses for incident response and forensics have received comparatively less attention and remain at an early stage. This work presents a systematic study of LLM-agent design for the forensic investigation of realistic web application attacks. We propose CyberSleuth, an autonomous agent that processes packet-level traces and application logs to identify the targeted service, the exploited vulnerability (CVE), and attack success. We evaluate the consequences of core design decisions - spanning tool integration and agent architecture - and provide interpretable guidance for practitioners. We benchmark four agent architectures and six LLM backends on 20 incident scenarios of increasing complexity, identifying CyberSleuth as the best-performing design. In a separate set of 10 incidents from 2025, CyberSleuth correctly identifies the exact CVE in 80% of cases. At last, we conduct a human study with 22 experts, which rated the reports of CyberSleuth as complete, useful, and coherent. They also expressed a slight preference for DeepSeek R1, a good news for open source LLM. To foster progress in defensive LLM research, we release both our benchmark and the CyberSleuth platform as a foundation for fair, reproducible evaluation of forensic agents. 1

## 1 Introduction

Cyber forensics is a cornerstone of modern cybersecurity operations. When a system is compromised, defenders must first identify which component was targeted, which vulnerability was exploited, and whether the attack ultimately succeeded. This critical investigation process remains heavily manual, time-consuming, and prone to error [4]. The growing complexity of webbased systems and the sophistication of attacks fur-

∗ Corresponding author: matteo.boffa@polito.it

1 Code: https://github.com/SmartData-Polito/LLM\_Agent\_ Cybersecurity\_Forensic

Kai Huang

Politecnico di Torino

Marco Mellia Politecnico di Torino

Matteo Boffa ∗ Politecnico di Torino

Zied Ben Houidi Huawei Technologies France

Dario Rossi Huawei Technologies France ther exacerbate the difficulty, leaving defenders at a disadvantage compared to increasingly automated adversaries.

Recent advances in LLMs have opened up promising directions for automating reasoning-heavy tasks [22]. LLM agents - models equipped with structured reasoning loops, tool use, and memory - have shown remarkable potential in diverse domains, from software engineering to scientific discovery [6, 30, 43]. In cybersecurity, initial research has focused mainly on red-team applications, where agents attack systems [11, 31], find vulnerabilities [42], and generate exploits [48]. In contrast, defensive (blue-team) LLM agents remain comparatively scarce [5, 46, 29] and are still at an early stage of development and evaluation [1], particularly for incident response and forensics.

In this paper, we introduce CyberSleuth, an autonomous LLM agent designed for the forensic investigation of realistic web application attacks. CyberSleuth autonomously analyses post-attack evidence (e.g., packet-level traces or application logs) and reconstructs the incident by (i) identifying the targeted application, (ii) determining the exploited vulnerability, i.e., the exact Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) used, and (iii) assessing whether the attack succeeded. Beyond simply producing a verdict, CyberSleuth generates structured, human-readable forensic reports to support Security Operations Centre (SOC) analysts in resolving incidents efficiently.

Designing such an agent entails addressing non-trivial challenges - memory management; orchestrating LLM reasoning [22]; selecting and integrating external tools; and balancing autonomy with reliability. Following recent work [31, 32], we adopt a MemGPT-style [27] multi-agent architecture in which specialised agents collaborate toward the overall goal.

To systematically explore these challenges, we present both a methodology and a benchmark for evaluating defensive LLM agents. Our benchmark consists of web-based forensic incidents of increasing complexity, ranging from simple path traversal attempts to multi-step exploitation chains, from old to freshly discovered vulnerabilities. This approach enables fair, reproducible comparison of different agent architectures

CORRECT ONE Figure 1: Overview of agent architectures. Each architecture receives traffic data (and logs) and processes it through a pipeline of pre-processing, reporting, and an LLM reasoning agent. The agents connect to the desired LLM backend via API, interact with tools and sub-agents ( tshark , Flow Reporter, Log Reporter and Web Search) to extract evidence and produce the forensic report.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

and LLM backends. To this aim, we release both CyberSleuth and the benchmark as an open platform for the community to establish a foundation for rigorous evaluation.

As shown in Fig. 1, we study four agent architectures. We improve CVE-identification accuracy from 28% to 68% (reaching 80% accuracy on 10 2025 incidents). Across six state-of-the-art LLM backends, GPT-5 and DeepSeek R1 perform best. Beyond aggregate metrics, we provide interpretable analyses linking specific design choices to these gains. Finally, we evaluate CyberSleuth 's report quality: according to 22 experts, reports are informative, complete, and helpful, with a slight preference for DeepSeek R1 (mean 4.39/5) - an encouraging result for open-source LLMs.

The key takeaways from the design of CyberSleuth are:

- A multi-agent design is essential : using subagents, experts in packet or log analysis, outperforms a single all-purpose agent, which gets lost in the data analysis.
- Simple orchestration beats nested designs : a straightforward pipeline of agents is more effective than architectures relying on deep inter-agent communication, which often leads to coordination failures.
- More data is not always better : finding the good balance on which data to process is fundamental. More data may distract the agent and worsen its performance.

Our study highlights both the promise and current limitations of defensive LLM agents, providing actionable insights for the design of future AI-powered forensic systems.

## 2 Related Works

With the rapid progress of LLMs, researchers have increasingly investigated their potential to support security experts. Prior work has mainly explored two directions: (i) using LLMs to defend systems by monitoring, reacting to, and reporting threats (blue-teaming), and (ii) simulating attacks to probe defences (red-teaming). Our work contributes to the blue-team line of research, while drawing methodological inspiration from the more mature red-teaming literature [1].

- Blue-team LLMs Most works in this space exploit the knowledge encoded in LLMs to automate specific tasks, while only a few attempt to design truly autonomous agents.

In the first category, CSEBenchmark [38] systematically evaluates 12 LLMs across 345 knowledge points expected from security experts. GPT- and DeepSeekbased models achieve the highest scores; yet all models perform poorly on procedural operations (e.g., using advanced tools such as tshark ). We observe the same limitation and propose mechanisms to mitigate it. Other studies leverage LLMs to parse verbose CTI reports into structured outputs, e.g., knowledge graph extraction [7], identification of Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) [8], or generation of incident reports - either autonomously or in collaboration with experts [12]. In contrast, our setting is more demanding: producing a report is only the final step of a broader forensic investigation. Nissist [3] orchestrates multiple LLMs in a semi-autonomous loop (planner, retriever, etc.) to mitigate incidents. However, unlike our work, the LLMs do not use tools and only process textual incident descriptions. Our agents must investigate directly from raw traces and logs.

Closer to our setting, a few works explore LLM-

1

agents for network forensics [5, 46, 29]. The agent in [5] is provided with pre-parsed payloads and metadata, whereas CyberSleuth autonomously extracts evidence from raw data. Similarly, [46] outputs qualitative outputs through a visual interface rather than structured forensic conclusions. CFABench [29] is the most similar work to CyberSleuth. It introduces a benchmark for forensic agents and evaluates three ReAct-style designs. We extend their benchmark and fairly compare against their best-performing agent, improving accuracy from 14% to 90% in the first stage of the investigation. Overall, existing works remain preliminary and lack the systematic design-space exploration that we conduct.

Finally, researchers have proposed LLM-agents for other blue-team tasks. Several focus on automating vulnerability patching [23, 44, 45], as in PATCHAGENT , which mimics how human experts triage bugs [45]. CyberSleuth follows a similar intuition, as we design it to mimic the steps a human analyst would take. Others investigate incident reporting and Root Cause Analysis (RCA) for server and cloud failures [41, 28, 40, 9]. These approaches share our idea of using LLMs to autonomously parse logs, but address benign system failures, whereas our focus is on adversarial scenarios requiring cybersecurity expertise.

· Red-team LLMs Red-teaming agents gained traction early, led by PentestGPT [11]. We borrow several methodological insights from this line of work. For instance, AutoPenBench [13] introduces step-wise metrics to track agent progress during penetration tests. Following the same principle, our evaluation is sequential : the agent must first identify the targeted service, then detect the vulnerability, and finally assess if the attack was successful.

Architectural innovations from red-team research also inform our design. Incalmo [32] separates highlevel reasoning from low-level execution, improving performance in multi-host CTFs. Similarly, VulnBot [15] orchestrates a team of specialised agents (planner, executor, analyser) to mitigate the 'loss of context' faced by single-agent models. Our advanced multi-agent variants follow a similar rationale.

· Benchmarking agentic solutions Several works stress best practices for evaluating LLMs in security. From Promise to Peril [1] highlights the need for explainability, human oversight, and resilience. OCCULT [16] cautions against binary yes/no metrics that inflate performance. Finally, [47] proposes two principles for agentic benchmarks: outcome validity (scores must reflect genuine success) and task validity (tasks must only be solvable if the capability is truly present). Our benchmark explicitly adheres to these guidelines by avoiding binary metrics, excluding unsolvable tasks, and ensuring human-interpretable reasoning traces.

## 3 LLM Agent Background

We introduce the LLM agent fundamentals here. For more details, we refer the reader to [22].

An agent is an autonomous system that interacts with its environment to achieve specific goals. It per- ceives inputs, processes information, and takes actions that influence the environment according to its objectives [34]. When powered by LLMs, agents leverage the model's reasoning and planning abilities to guide decision-making. The agent core components are:

- Reasoning &amp; Decision-Making: The LLM performs iterative reasoning steps, analyses observations, formulates intermediate thoughts, and decides the next action.
- Actions: Allow the agents to interact with the environment through predefined tools , i.e., executing applications, querying APIs, or updating internal memory.
- Memory: Agents use short-term memory for immediate interactions, and long-term memory to retain past knowledge.

The agent is programmed via a prompt which defines the task to achieve, the tools the agent can use, and the input data (which accumulates over interactions). The agent reasons and decides which actions to perform next, runs them, and observes the environment's reaction to such actions. Through repeated cycles of perception, reasoning, and action, LLM agents autonomously tackle complex tasks that would be infeasible to script manually.

Importantly, the prompt size is limited by the LLM's Context Window 2 , which limits the amount of text (thoughts, actions, observations, memory) that can be processed at once. Effective context window management is therefore critical: saturation may cause omission of relevant information and potentially prevent the agent from completing its tasks.

Short-term memory is contained in the prompt and, therefore, is limited. Long-term memory allows the agent to store and retrieve information from previous interactions in an external database [22], thus overcoming this limitation.

LLM-powered systems can be extended to multiagent architectures, where multiple sub-agents specialise in different aspects of a task and collaborate, coordinated by a main agent . Each agent may focus on a specific role, e.g, one dedicated to evidence collection, another to vulnerability analysis, and a third to report generation. Multi-agent setups enable parallel reasoning, reduce cognitive load on individual agents, and can improve robustness by cross-validating observations and decisions.

To summarise, tool design, multi-agent coordination and short/long memory management are keys to the success of the agent.

## 4 CyberSleuth: Forensics LLMs

We design and evaluate four LLM agents that autonomously analyse post-attack evidence in the form of packet-level traces and system logs.

2 Each LLM has a different context window measured in tokens, the fundamental unit of text that the model uses to process and generate language.

Fig. 1 provides an overview of the design choices and illustrates the key differences across architectures.

At input, the agent receives data collected during an attack on a victim system, including raw packet traces and application logs generated by the targeted service(s) (see Sec. 6 for details). At output, the agent must (i) identify the targeted service, (ii) determine the CVE exploited by the attacker, (iii) assess whether the attack was successful, and (iv) produce a structured forensic report summarising its findings.

In this paper, we compare four agent architectures that differ in how they process evidence and structure reasoning. All agents share the same three-stage pipeline, including pre-processing, reporting and main agent execution, that we sketch in Fig. 1. Also, agents share the same memory management mechanism, a common task prompt, and access to the same specialised Web Search tool. Their differences lie in the input data they exploit, the analysis tools they employ, and whether they adopt a single- or multi-agent structure.

We implement all agents using LangChain [18] and LangGraph [19], a graph-based framework for agent development. Graph nodes correspond to reasoning steps or tool invocations. The execution flow is dynamically determined by the LLM, which at each step may continue reasoning, invoke a tool, or finalise the report. The agent updates its memory after every step and uses it to inform subsequent decisions. The agent connects to the desired LLM backend via API.

By contrasting these four designs, we systematically explore how different architectural choices affect forensic performance. This comparison allows us to isolate which design features - such as richer evidence sources, specialised tools, or multi-agent structures - contribute most to improving accuracy and reliability.

In the following, we detail the common part first, and present each agent. We highlight prompts and actual shell commands using different background colour.

## 4.1 Memory Management Structure

Prior efforts that applied LLM agents to forensic tasks [29] adopted the simple ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) framework, where the agent alternates between reasoning and acting (external tool execution). The result of each action is concatenated in a scratchpad (i.e. the short-term memory) that allows the agent to augment the information at its disposal. However, because the scratchpad contributes to the LLM's context window, long sequences of thoughts, actions, and observations can quickly saturate available context, dropping the oldest information. Even worse, if the input data already exceeds the context window.

To mitigate this issue, we take inspiration from the recently introduced MemGPT -style memory management [27]. We introduce an external Vector Database (i.e. its long-term memory) to store information. The agent has a specialised tool to interact with it. We augment the agent with the ability to manage its memory by partitioning the prompt into:

- System Instructions : Contains the static definitions of the agent's role, available tools, tasks, and specialised memory management capabilities.
- Working Context : Is dynamically populated with information the agent retrieves from the longterm memory.
- FIFO Queue : Is a token-budgeted buffer holding the most recent actions and observations. This is the traditional short-term memory (similar to the scratchpad).

At each iteration, the agent computes embeddings of the last few FIFO queue entries and retrieves semantically related entries from the vector database. It inserts them into the Working Context. This mechanism enables the agent to preserve coherence, recall relevant past insights, and avoid redundancy across long forensic investigations. The agent autonomously decides which content to store in the vector database using the specialised memory management tool.

## 4.2 Task and Prompt

All agents are guided by a common description of the task specified in the System Instructions section of the prompt. Below we report the baseline version, which implements the Single Agent (see Sec.3) : ✞ ROLE: You are a specialised network forensics analyst. You are working towards the final task in a step-by-step manner.

## INSTRUCTION:

You are provided with a queue of the most recent steps in the reasoning process. Store the most relevant information as soon as you get it, because the queue has a limited size and older messages will be removed.

By thinking in a step-by-step manner, provide only one single reasoning step in response to the last observation and the action for the next step.

## TOOLS:

You have tools available to search online for CVEs and extract frames from the pcap file.

When you are ready to provide the final answer, stop the reasoning and format the result.

CONTEXT: Analyse the provided PCAP (Packet Capture) file to accomplish the following tasks:

1. Identify the name of the service or application involved.

2. Determine the relevant CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and

Exposures) based on the captured data.

3. Gather evidence of malicious activities associated with the identified CVE.
4. Assess whether the service or application is vulnerable to the identified attack.
5. Evaluate whether the attack was successful.

## GUIDELINES:

- Begin by thoroughly analysing the PCAP file to extract relevant information.
- After the exploratory analysis, ensure that the CVE identification is accurate by cross-referencing details from external sources with the evidence found in the PCAP files.
- Use the online search tool only after the exploratory analysis has been completed to verify the findings and gather additional information.

✝ The prompt specifies the agent's role, instructions for memory management, the available tools, the forensic context and tasks, and high-level guidelines for execution. We refined and improved the prompt wording

☎

✆

for conciseness and clarity, ensuring the agent receives explicit, structured instructions while minimising ambiguity. We found the so-called 'prompt engineering' to be important, but not any more a fundamental part of the agent design.

## 4.3 Tool: Focussed Web Search

It is dedicated to retrieving and aggregating online information about known vulnerabilities (CVEs). The main agent formulates a natural-language query, which the Web Search Tool uses to query the web via the Google Custom Search API [14]. The tool retrieves the top 10 results ranked by Google, visits each page and summarises it, finally adding a summary to extract the relevant content using the following prompt:

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

☎

✆

```
✞ You are an AI assistant tasked with summarising content relevant to '{query}' for a forensic analyst who is trying to identify the CVE related to a specific service/application under analysis. Please provide a concise summary in {character_limit} characters or less where you highlight your findings for each CVE detected in the web page. The summary should be in the following form for each CVE identified: 'CVE-XXXX-YYYY: Description of the CVE and its relevance to the service/application under analysis.' ✝
```

The resulting summaries are returned to the main agent, which can then cross-check and consolidate the evidence with information extracted from local traces and logs.

We found it essential to constrain the Web Search Tool to produce concise summaries rather than passing full web pages to the main agent. This design minimises the risk of the agent being distracted by irrelevant details or hallucinating facts not grounded in the evidence. By focusing only on short, structured summaries of CVE-related findings, the agent can reason more reliably and efficiently. 3

This design enables the agent to ground its forensic reasoning in both local evidence and external, up-todate vulnerability knowledge.

## 5 Agent Architectures

We incrementally explore different agent design options, presenting three agent architectures sketched in Fig. 1. All agents share the same MemGTP-style memory management and can invoke the Web Search Tool. Their main differences lies in how they access and browse the input data collected during the incident. We consider only the packet trace, provided as a raw PCAP file, as a starting point; later, we explore the benefits of accessing the system logs. For the sake of brevity, we provide the prompt of each agent and sub-agent in Appendix D.

## 5.1 Single Agent (SA)

The first architecture is a straightforward implementation of a single agent, equivalent to the one introduced

3 In [29], unconstrained use of the web search tool was a major factor behind poor performance.

in [29]. It emulates the behaviour of a solitary security analyst proficient in packet analysis with tools such as Wireshark and tshark . Given the packet list summary , the agent interacts with the environment and external sources through two tools: the PCAP Reader and the Web Search Tool .

Tool: PCAP Reader It is a simple wrapper around tshark that provides the core function to access the complete information of a given packet: ✞

☎

tshark -r {pcap\_file} -Y "frame.number=={frame\_number}" -T fields -e data ✝

✆

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

☎

Operational Workflow As shown in the Fig. 1 top block, we initially populate the SA agent memory with the packet list summary of the entire trace obtained by running tshark : ✞ tshark -r {pcap\_file} -T fields -e frame.number -e frame.time -e frame.protocols -e \_ws.col.Info ✝ This generates a summary of all packets, including Frame ID, timestamp, highest-level protocol, and a concise information field. Then, the SA autonomously analyzes this summary to identify packets of interest, using PCAP Reader for deeper inspection, or Web Search Tool to access external knowledge. It stores intermediate reasoning results in the FIFO queue to preserve short-term context across steps and progressively consolidates them into long-term memory. The process iterates until the forensic task is completed or the maximum reasoning depth is reached.

✆

## 5.2 Tshark Expert Agent (TEA)

A key limitation of SA is that, when an attack spans hundreds of thousands of packets, even the input packet list summary can saturate the LLM's context window. To overcome this, we propose TEA, a nested multiagent architecture. The main agent acts as a forensic network analyst: it first extracts all TCP and UDP connections , then performs a fine-grained inspection of the relevant portions of the trace. To keep the main agent focused on reasoning, TEA introduces a dedicated tshark sub-agent , which replaces the limited PCAP Reader tool. By delegating execution to the specialised sub-agent with expert knowledge of tshark , we reduce syntax errors, avoid irrelevant operations, and improve the precision of forensic analysis.

The tshark sub-agent i) receives high-level natural language instructions from the main agent; ii) translates them into executable tshark commands to query the PCAP file; iii) returns concise summaries of the extracted evidence. Meanwhile, the main agent retains access to the Web Search Tool to gather complementary external information.

Tshark Sub-agent We implement the Tshark subagent with a modular structure, mirroring the design of the main agent. Upon receiving a natural language instruction, it leverages two tools: the Tshark Manual Reader and the Tshark Executor tools.

- Tool: Tshark Manual Reader is a semantic search tool built from the official tshark documentation [36] and filter guide [37]. It allows the subagent to retrieve the most relevant manual sections given the main agent's instruction, ensuring accurate and context-aware command generation.
- Tool: Tshark Executor is a command execution tool that runs the generated tshark commands, analyses the outputs, automatically detects and corrects errors, refines filters, truncates results to respect constraints.

The sub-agent iteratively refines and executes commands, producing a structured summary of results once the analysis is complete or it reaches a maximum iteration threshold.

☎

✆

☎

Operational Workflow As shown in the second block of Fig. 1, TEA receives the PCAP trace and the list of connections extracted using: ✞ tshark -r &lt;pcap\_file&gt; -q -z conv,tcp -z conv,udp ✝ whose output is embedded in TEA's initial prompt. It then begins the reasoning process. When it identifies connections requiring deeper inspection, it delegates the task to the Tshark sub-agent via a natural language instruction, for instance: ✞ Follow stream n. {XX} in the PCAP file and report relevant details. ✝ The Tshark Agent interprets the request, supported by the Manual Reader Tool . It generates and executes the appropriate commands with the Executor Tool , and finally returns a concise summary of the findings along with the executed command history for completeness.

✆

The Web Search Tool and memory management work as usual.

## 5.3 Flow Reporter Agent (FRA)

We design a simple sequential multi-agent system. FRA architecture takes inspiration from the workflow of a human cybersecurity analyst who i) inspects the PCAP files and ii) analyses in depth the results to detect anomalies. This shifts the role of the main agent from exploration to interpretation.

To this end, we design a Flow Summariser subagent . It systematically inspects connections in the input file and generates a structured report summarising the most important forensic indicators, such as unusual ports, anomalous payload sizes or suspicious timing patterns. This enables a thorough traffic inspection and provides a targeted context for the main agent. The main agent analyses the report and performs the forensic investigation, only relying on the Web Search Tool .

Flows Summariser Sub-agent is a dedicated agent that is proficient in inspecting trace files. For each connection in a PCAP file, it reconstructs the complete application-layer exchange using tshark to extract the ASCII payload and create a text representation:

☎

✞ tshark -r &lt;pcap\_file&gt; -q -z follow,{tcp|udp}, ascii, {stream\_number} ✝ Armed with all connection data, we task the Flows Summariser sub-agent to generate a structured forensic report describing:

✆

- Service : The service(s) involved and, if possible, the version to support accurate web queries.
- Relevant Events : Summary of the most important actions of the IP addresses involved and, optionally, quotes the content of the traffic to support interpretation.
- Malicious Activities : Identifies suspicious behaviour and assigns it to the corresponding service.
- Attack Success : Indicates whether an attack occurred and whether it was successful.

To save tokens and time, we avoid calling the Flows Summariser sub-agent for TLS-encrypted connections because they contain little information and can hinder the analysis.

Given that the payload of a connection can quickly become very large and overflow the LLM context window, we introduce a constraint to control the amount of payload to pass to the LLM. To maintain a broad coverage of all connections, we apply a token budgeting strategy. Given an estimate of the total number of tokens that the entire payload would consume, we apply a token allocation strategy based on square root allocation. For each connection i , compute:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

final allocation i = min(tokens i , allocation i )

This prevents large connections from consuming a disproportionately large part of the context budget and, at the same time, ensures that smaller but potentially important connections are analysed.

The Flows Summariser Agent allocates half of the tokens to the beginning and half to the end of the connection. This strategy increases the likelihood of capturing important forensic indicators that are typically located at session boundaries, such as attack signatures or service banners. 4

Operational Workflow As shown in the Fig. 1 third block, TRA gets as input the same list of connections provided to TEA. The Flow Summariser sub-agent gets this list and inspects each connection individually to create its summary. The main agent receives the summary and proceeds with its forensic investigation, reasoning, using the Web Search Tool , and managing its memory.

## 5.4 Log Summariser-enabled Agents

We further extend the agent architectures with the capability to process application logs. Directly exposing

4 We tested block chunking, overlapping-window chunking, linear allocation strategy. The root-square allocation resulted in the most robust one.

the full log set to the LLM could quickly exceed the LLM context window and pollute the prompt with unrelated information. Instead, we handle the log collection and generate a concise, forensic-oriented summary. Access to logs is optional, as part of our evaluation considers the agent's ability to investigate incidents using only the PCAP trace. In addition, logs vary greatly in quality: some are sparse and uninformative, while others are bloated with redundant or low-value entries. To handle this heterogeneity, we design a specialised sub-agent dedicated to extracting high-level forensic evidence from logs. This component can be coupled with any of the previously described architectures. Fig. 1 (bottom block) illustrates its use in combination with the Flow Reporter Agent architecture.

Log Summariser sub-agent produces an actionable synthesis of raw logs. In line with the Flows Reporter Summariser , its output must report the services and applications involved (including version information) when available, and highlight anomalous, suspicious, or error-related events. Each finding must be supported with references to specific log entries to preserve traceability. To mitigate speculative reasoning, the Log Summariser must also return an explicit statement when no relevant suspicious activity is detected.

Finally, we note that the Log Summariser can also be interpreted as a standalone agent relying exclusively on system logs. Its standalone performance, however, is generally poor, reflecting the inherent limitations of logs, which often lack the granularity and evidence carried by network traffic traces.

## 6 Benchmark and Metrics

## 6.1 Benchmark Datasets

Here we describe the benchmarking scenarios, data and metrics we use to compare and choose the best performing agent.

Agent Design Dataset We build on CFAbench [29]. The original benchmark consists 20 web-based incidents targeting containerised services collected in a controlled testbed. Each incident involves an external attacker attempting to exploit a specific CVE of the given service. 15 of these attacks are conducted by malicious HTTP requests (RESTAPI, buffer overflow, etc.). Others involve non-web protocols. Services include databases, middleware, web servers, etc. 14 attacks succeeded (vulnerable system), while the 6 failed (either patched systems, or vulnerable component disabled).

The benchmark provides network traces and logs for each incident. The traffic traces are not encrypted and include only communication between the service and the attacker. 5 The traffic volume of different incidents

5 We assume traffic traces are collected after TLS termination, and that the traffic related to the incident has already been extracted.

varies greatly, ranging from a dozen to thousands of packets and megabytes of data. Logs vary even more, with incidents with no data, and others with hundreds of mostly unrelated information. In only 4 incidents, logs provide evidence of the service and attack. We use these 20 incidents to estimate agent performance to guide its design and LLM selection.

Independent Testing Dataset We extend the testbed provided by CFA-bench and collect 10 additional incidents for independent testing. To challenge the agents to solve incidents with information released after the LLMs training, we select five services (4 HTTP-, 1 SSH-based) affected by vulnerabilities newly disclosed in 2025. We deploy both a vulnerable and a patched version of the service and attack both.

For each incident, Tab. 1 reports details from both CFA-bench benchmark and our test set. The CFAbench incidents are sorted based on the ability of the agents to solve them, from the easy to the unsolved ones.

Benign Browsing Dataset For completeness, we collect 10 incidents which involve regular web-browsing activities related to popular services login, web searches, browsing sessions, etc. They contain no attack. In two login attempts only, we entered few times wrong username/passwords. 6 . We challenge CyberSleuth with this data to observe how it behaves with benign traffic and see if it is prone to flag false positives.

## 6.2 Benchmark Metrics

We measure agents' abilities to correctly solve the incidents and provide some metrics related to their internal functioning.

Agent Performance Metrics We follow the metrics defined by CFA-bench that mimic how a security analyst would evaluate an incident. The goal is to generate a textual report that describes the incident. 7 Each report includes 3 checkpoints that also serve as sub-tasks to assess the report correctness. The checkpoints are true/false tests:

- Service Identification : Did the agent correctly identify the service under attack?
- CVE detection : Was the specific vulnerability (CVE number) correctly identified?
- Attack Success Evaluation : Did the agent correctly assess whether the attack was successful?

In the attack success evaluation, the agent faces a binary classification task. Since the test set is imbalanced (14 successful vs. 6 failed attacks), a naive classifier that always predicts 'success' would already achieve

6 We collect traces enabling the SSLKEYLOGFILE to collect the TLS keys and passing those to the agents to decrypt the payload.

7 We do not report the Vulnerable Service metric [29] because it can be computed only if the agent has correctly identified both the service and CVE. Otherwise, it is random guess.

Table 1: List of incidents for benchmark. Bottom ones are the new incidents we collected.

|   ID | Service            | Version   | CVE            |   # Containers | Success   |   # Packets | Traffic volume   | Log # Lines   |
|------|--------------------|-----------|----------------|----------------|-----------|-------------|------------------|---------------|
|    0 | Apache Solr        | 8.11.0    | CVE-2021-44228 |              1 | True      |          22 | 2.89KB           | 156 † ⋆       |

|   13 | Joomla             | 4.2.7     | CVE-2023-23752 |              1 | True      |         596 | 205.09KB         | 14            |

|   19 | Apache APISIX      | 2.11      | CVE-2021-45232 |              3 | False     |         500 | 74.03KB          | 99 †          |

|   21 | Vite               | 6.2.3     | CVE-2025-30208 |              1 | False     |          24 | 3.48KB           | 14 †          |
|   22 | Next.js            | 15.2.2    | CVE-2025-29927 |              1 | True      |          26 | 11.54KB          | 9 †           |
|   23 | Next.js            | 15.2.3    | CVE-2025-29927 |              1 | False     |          24 | 2.46KB           | 10 †          |
|   24 | Langflow           | 1.2.0     | CVE-2025-3248  |              1 | True      |          20 | 2.18KB           | 18 †          |
|   25 | Langflow           | 1.2.0     | CVE-2025-3248  |              1 | True      |         135 | 121.01KB         | 18 †          |
|   26 | Langflow           | 1.5.0     | CVE-2025-3248  |              1 | False     |          44 | 6.23KB           | 405 †         |
|   27 | Tomcat             | 9.0.97    | CVE-2025-24813 |              1 | False     |          54 | 29.59KB          | 40 †          |
|   28 | Erlang/OTP         | 27.3.2    | CVE-2025-32433 |              1 | True      |          20 | 2.97KB           | 2             |
|   29 | Erlang/OTP         | 27.3.3    | CVE-2025-32433 |              1 | False     |          19 | 2.88KB           | 2             |

an accuracy of 14 / 20 = 70%. To provide a more informative assessment, we therefore report also the F1 score and the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) [21]. The latter offers a balanced measure of performance under class imbalance, with values in the range [ -1 , 1]: a score of 1 indicates perfect prediction, 0 corresponds to random guessing, and -1 denotes total disagreement between predictions and ground truth.

Agent Internal Metrics reflect the efficiency of each agent. Specifically, we record the number of reasoning steps taken before termination; the number of input and output tokens processed by the LLM; and the associated monetary cost for each run (obtained from the cloud platform directly). These metrics measure the amount of information that the agent processes and generates before reaching its conclusions. Tokens directly affect the cost. Input tokens are less expensive than output tokens for cloud-based LLMs.

## 6.3 Human Report Assessment

We pooled human experts to evaluate the quality of the final report and provide a qualitative assessment of agent performance. We ask experts to evaluate three aspects on a scale from 0 to 5 (0 - Totally Unsatisfied, 5 - Totally Satisfied):

- Completeness : Does the report include relevant and accurate information?

· Usefulness : Is the report helpful to a human analyst?

- Logical Coherence : Is the agent's reasoning clear and logically consistent?

We involve 22 volunteers. We select four incidents from the 2025 testset in which the agent was successful and show the agent's reasoning steps, the final report and the PCAP file. The volunteers self-declared their security forensic expertise and proceeded to compile an online questionnaire. 8

## 7 Results

In this section, we systematically assess the agent design options for CyberSleuth. After describing the experimental setting, we select the best agent architecture considering only the PCAP as input, compare different LLMs, and observe the benefits in adding the system logs.

For our experiments, we use well-known LLMs exposed through public interfaces 9 , 10 . We evaluate four architectures, two input data, and six different LLMs. For our experiments, we keep the default LLMs' temperatures 11 , and repeat three times each incident to account for nondeterminism. The time to complete an incident analysis varies from a few to 15-20 minutes.

8 Questionnaire available at https://forms.gle/ QsaJ1dVUSK7suu3K6

9 https://platform.openai.com

10 https://www.together.ai/

11 Parameter that regulates the model generation randomness. Ranges from 0 to 1, where 0 corresponds to a deterministic generation. Empirical evidence [20, 39] shows that deterministic generations can impair performance.

Table 2: Comparison of different agent architectures. best in bold ; second-best underlined.

| Metric              | CFA-bench   | [29]   | SA    | TEA   | FRA   |
|---------------------|-------------|--------|-------|-------|-------|
| Service ( ↑ )       | 0.42        |        | 0.45  | 58.33 | 66.67 |
| CVE ( ↑ )           | 0.14        |        | 0.28  | 35.00 | 45.00 |
| Acc ( ↑ )           | 0.13        |        | 0.47  | 48.33 | 61.67 |
| F1( ↑ )             | -           |        | 0.49  | 46.00 | 62.00 |
| MCC ( ↑ )           | -           |        | -0.11 | 0.00  | 0.45  |
| Success Steps ( ↓ ) | -           |        | 18.11 | 11.32 | 5.48  |
| In. Tokens ( ↓ )    | -           |        | 5.48M | 4.37M | 3.86M |
| Out. Tokens ( ↓ )   | -           |        | 78k   | 123k  | 112k  |
| Cost [ $ ] ( ↓ )    | -           |        | 14.48 | 12.15 | 10.78 |

## 7.1 Which Agent Architecture

We evaluate the performance of the first three agent architectures shown in Fig. 1. We keep the back-end LLM fixed to OpenAI GPT-4o and set the maximum number of steps to 25. We summarise results in Tab. 2.

Best Architecture: Start with the Single Agent (SA) and compare its performance against the CFAbench baseline. Both approaches achieve similar accuracy in identifying the service under attack. However, thanks to the improved Web Search tool and the MemGPT-style memory management, SA achieves substantially better results in other dimensions: CVE identification improves by +14%, and the attack success evaluation by +34%. These gains are attributed to SA's ability to (i) retrieve better targeted information about candidate CVEs from the Web, and (ii) accumulate and reuse contextual information over multiple reasoning steps to refine its conclusions.

Despite these improvements, the overall SA performance remains unsatisfactory. Notably, the high number of steps reflects the SA difficulties in using tshark efficiently: it sequentially inspects packets until it finds some evidence, causing a waste of input tokens, and finally loosing focus. Additionally, the MCC is negative, indicating that its predictions are biased in the wrong direction - worse than random guessing. This effect stems from SA's tendency to return 'attack unsuccessful' whenever it fails to gather sufficient evidence. Because the benchmark is unbalanced (14 successes versus 6 failures), this systematically biases SA's predictions toward the wrong class, explaining its poor MCC despite reasonable accuracy and F1 scores (recall - a random guess would have 50% accuracy).

Next, we analyse the performance of the Tshark Expert Agent (TEA) and Flow Reporter Agent (FRA) architectures. Both outperform SA in identifying the correct service. However, only FRA achieves a consistent improvement in CVE detection and attack success evaluation. The limited TEA gains can be traced back to a coordination gap between the main agent and the Tshark sub-agent . As shown in Appendix B, the main agent often issues overly broad requests (e.g., 'explore the HTTP traffic'), which triggers the Tshark sub-agent into performing inconclusive analyses, eventually losing direction. As a result, many runs fail to converge to useful evidence. Nonetheless, when Tshark sub-agent succeeds in extracting a meaningful clue, TEA is able to resolve the task efficiently. For instance, TEA cor- rectly identifies the service in 35 runs out of 60, and in 21 of these it also pinpoints the correct CVE. Once the service is known, the CVE is typically identified within just three reasoning steps - in a (query → reasoning → second query) pattern. This shows that, although the TEA design provides fine-grained packet analysis capabilities, its practical effectiveness is hampered by coordination issues between its two agent layers.

Figure 2: Breakdown of performance in Service (top) and CVE (bottom) identification over the 20 incidents.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

The FRA architecture resolves the coordination gap by decoupling the Flow Summariser sub-agent from the main agent. The summariser independently conducts a systematic analysis of the PCAP, producing a high-quality report of suspicious activities without being influenced by intermediate reasoning steps. The main agent then refines these findings through targeted reasoning and web searches. Although this limits the agent's action space since it cannot query the summariser again, FRA achieves the best overall performance at the lowest cost. On average, it converges in just ∼ 5 steps, significantly reducing input token usage. Notice the positive MCC testifying that it takes wellgrounded decisions based on the evidence collected by the Flow Summariser sub-agent.

For these reasons, we select FRA as the architecture underpinning CyberSleuth.

Result Breakdown Fig. 2 reports, for each incident in the dataset, how many times (out of three runs) the agents correctly identify the service (top) and the CVE (bottom). Incidents are ordered by difficulty. SA can correctly identify the service of the first 8 incidents only. In 5 of them, the agent immediately guesses the exact service at the first reasoning step. In fact, for those incidents, the answer is self-contained in the packet list summary . As previously stated, once the service is found, the agent can get the correct CVE with one or two web searches. In contrast, when finding the service requires using the PCAP reader tool , the reasoning trace becomes much longer (18 steps on average, Table 2), a clear sign that the agent starts losing focus and finally fails in both tasks. TEAshows improved service identification thanks to the TShark sub-agent , but

the coordination issues discussed earlier often prevent it from translating partial findings into successful CVE detection. By contrast, FRA's decoupling strategy ensures more consistent progress: the Flow Summariser provides focused evidence, enabling the main agent to refine its reasoning and achieve higher accuracy in both service and CVE identification.

Looking at the hardest incidents (15-19), these involve multi-container systems where the attack generates a large volume of traffic (Tab. 5), causing the agents to lose focus. Incident 17-Cacti is a notable exception: here, the difficulty stems not from traffic overload but from the scarcity of online information. In this scenario, the Web Search tool provides little support, and agents ultimately fail. Conversely, TEA and FRA fail to identify the CVE in incidents 7 and 8. These incidents involve Apache ActiveMQ exposing OS and kernel versions in plain text. The attacker simply uses a telnet connection to issue a standard HTTP GET request, leaving almost no forensic evidence of malicious activity. This lack of distinctive signals hampers the coordination between the Tshark sub-agent (in TEA) or the Flow Summariser (in FRA) and the main agent.

At last, we observe the impact of the randomness in the LLM's reasoning abilities. Despite this being a common problem [27], we expect different LLMs to offer different performance (see Sec. 7.2).

Web Search Tool Usage We further analyse how agents leverage the Web Search tool by examining a subset of increasingly complex incidents (Fig. 3). Each row reports the last query issued in one run, with cell colours reflecting whether the correct CVE was identified (green) or not (red).

We identify three typical failures: (A) query asking for the wrong service, (B) query broadly looking for attacks, and (C) query asking for the wrong attack. At large, a 'correct' query does not guarantee success, and a wrong query implies failure. For instance, service misidentification (A) makes the whole analysis fail (see SA looking for JetDirect and etcd in the Couch DB and APISIX incidents). In the Gitlab incidents, SA gets lost in investigating the long packet list, reaching the maximum step number. TEA performs slightly better, e.g., correctly looking for CouchDB . Yet, its queries are too broad to succeed in complex scenarios. Only FRA manages to recover CVEs for some challenging incidents. Curiously, in the 12-APISIX incident, queries are correct, and the true CVE is present in the web search results. Yet the agent picks the right CVE in only one run, suggesting that GPT-4o struggles at interpreting web search results.

## 7.2 Which LLM Backend

We study the effects of different back-end LLMs on the performance while providing some quantitative interpretation of their reasoning. We test only the FRA architecture and compare six LLMs.

In Tab. 3 we detail the results. Focusing first on the closed-source OpenAI models, both o3 and GPT-5

Table 3: Study over different LLMs.

| Metric              | OpenAI GPT-4o [26]   | OpenAI o3 [25]   | OpenAI GPT-5 [24]   | Deepseek R1 [10]   | Kimi K2 [35]   | LLama-4 Maverik [2]   |
|---------------------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Service ( ↑ )       | 0.67                 | 0.75             | 0.80                | 0.85               | 0.82           | 0.75                  |
| CVE ( ↑ )           | 0.45                 | 0.48             | 0.68                | 0.67               | 0.63           | 0.53                  |
| Acc ( ↑ )           | 0.62                 | 0.80             | 0.78                | 0.78               | 0.78           | 0.60                  |
| F1( ↑ )             | 0.62                 | 0.79             | 0.77                | 0.76               | 0.76           | 0.63                  |
| MCC ( ↑ )           | 0.45                 | 0.65             | 0.63                | 0.55               | 0.55           | 0.35                  |
| Success Steps ( ↓ ) | 5.48                 | 3.55             | 3.93                | 5.42               | 6.70           | 17.12                 |
| In. Tokens ( ↓ )    | 3.86M                | 3.67M            | 3.92M               | 4.66M              | 4.35M          | 8.69M                 |
| Out. Tokens ( ↓ )   | 112k                 | 245k             | 373k                | 555k               | 94k            | 215k                  |
| Cost [ $ ] ( ↓ )    | 8.60                 | 9.30             | 8.63                | 3.78               | 4.63           | 2.52                  |

outperform the baseline GPT-4o in service identification (+8% and +13%, respectively). This aligns with expectations: o3 is a dedicated reasoning model , designed to generate an internal reasoning trace before producing the final answer, at the cost of higher latency. This makes it well-suited for detailed forensic investigations. GPT-5 , in contrast, is a hybrid model that can dynamically invoke reasoning when needed and benefits from a more recent training process. For CVE detection, however, o3 performs only on par with GPT-4o . As we will show in Fig. 4, this is due to o3 often being overconfident in its own knowledge and relying less on the Web Search tool. Still, thanks to its reasoning capabilities, o3 achieves the highest performance in attack success evaluation (0.64 MCC), even surpassing the newer GPT-5 . Both models also require fewer reasoning steps on average, but their verbosity makes the overall cost comparable to GPT-4o . In Appendix C, we provide a practical example of how GPT-5 can improve the performance of GPT-4o .

Turning to open-source models, both DeepSeek R1 and Kimi-K2 12 deliver impressive results, matching or surpassing OpenAI's best models across most metrics. This is particularly encouraging, as they can be deployed on-premise at inference time, avoiding the need to outsource sensitive data. Even when accessed via the cloud (as in our experiments), these models remain significantly cheaper than their closed-source counterparts-roughly half the cost. The open source Llama-4 Maverick improves over GPT-4o , but ranks only second to last overall.

In short, open-source LLMs rival or even exceed the performance of the best proprietary models, while offering greater flexibility and substantially lower cost.

Result Breakdown To provide a more interpretable view of model performance, Fig. 4 analyses CVE identification outcomes across all 60 runs (20 incidents, each repeated 3 times). For each model, we report the fraction of runs: i) where the model issues a web query (green bar); ii) where the last web response contains the correct CVE identifier (blue bar); iii) the final CVE successfully detected, distinguishing correct answers supported by web evidence (cream bars) from those obtained through prior knowledge (red bar).

All models use the Web Search tool in over 80% of cases, except o3 , which does so in only 50% of runs. This reflects its distinctive behaviour: despite the prompt explicitly encouraging web searches when

12 Open-source models make their code, architecture, and trained weights freely available for use, modification, and redistribution.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

A: Wrong service identification

B: Broad attack identification

C: Wrong attack identification

D: Run failed - Max Steps

B: Broad attack query

C: Wrong attack query

D: Run failed - Max steps

A: Wrong service query

Figure 3: Agent's web searches in increasingly complex incidents. Red cells indicate that the agent failed in CVE identification.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 4: Detailed outcomes of web searches and CVE identification across 60 runs.The sum of CVE detection success rates corresponds to the accuracy values of Table 3.

uncertain (see Appendix D), o3 tends to rely heavily on its internal knowledge, often displaying overconfidence. The quality of the queries is captured by the blue bar. Here, GPT-4o performs poorly: it searches in 95% of runs, yet only 54% of responses contain the correct CVE. By contrast, Llama-4 Maverick emerges as the most effective searcher: it always queries the web, and 80% of its results include the correct CVE. The crucial step is then selecting the right CVE among candidates. GPT-5 , DeepSeek R1 , and Kimi K2 excel here: whenever the correct CVE is present, they pick it 90-95% of the time (blue bar ≈ cream bar). Llama-4 Maverick , however, struggles to distinguish between near-identical CVEs, choosing the wrong one in 30% of such cases showing a clear reasoning limitation. Finally, the red bar highlights cases where the correct CVE is predicted without it appearing in web search results, i.e., by relying on LLM's prior knowledge. As said, o3 frequently succeeds this way. Interestingly, GPT-5 shows a more systematic use of external evidence, only occasionally complementing it with prior knowledge.

Overall, the figure reveals three distinct behaviours: o3 relies primarily on offline priors; GPT-5 and DeepSeek R1 combine web evidence with strong reasoning in a balanced manner; Llama-4 Maverick and GPT-4o suffer from mismatches between retrieval and reasoning.

|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------|----------------|
|                     | FRA             | FRA + LRA       | FRA            | FRA + LRA      |

| CVE ( ↑ )           | 0.45            | 0.50            | 0.68           | 0.65           |
